content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814
January 7, 1977

Floyd Shimoaura
Deputy Attorney General
555 Capitol Mall, Suite 550
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: CV 76/236 IL

Dear Floyd:

This letter is to confirm Ted Prim's conversation
with you on December 23, 1976, concerning Attorney General
Opinion request CV 76/236 IL. As he mentioned, we request
that your opinion, or at least those portions which discuss
the Political Reform Act, not be published. The comments
which follow discuss the opinion request by Assemblyman
Brown only as they relate to the Political Reform Act. Our
office has no comment relative to the application of Government Code Section 8920.1/

The questions as we understand them are as follows:

Assemblyman Brown wishes to know whether he may
represent two physicians before a state board
which will consider revocation of the physician's
licenses and cancellation of their Medi-Cal providers certificates. If Assemblyman Brown is
prohibited from representing the physicians, may
another member of the Assemblyman's firm represent
them? If so, must assemblyman Brown amend the law
or his partnership agreement in order to guarantee
that he will receive no portion of the physician's
payment to the firm.

Section 87100 provides:

No public official at any level of state or local
government shall make, participate in making or in
any way attempt to use his official position to
influence a governmental decision in which he
knows or has reason to know he has a financial
interest.

Emphasis added.

1/ All references are to the Government Code
unless otherwise noted.

Floyd Shimonura
January 7, 1977
Page Two

2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700(c) states that:

"In any way attempting to use his or her official
position to influence a governmental decision"
shall include furthering or attempting to affect
in any manner any decision:

(1) within or before his or her agency; or

(2) Before any agency which is appointed by
or subject to the budgetary control of his or
her agency.

Emphasis added.

Since the legislature has budgetary control over the board
in question, Assemblyman Brown may not use his official
position to influence decisions of the board in which he has
a financial interest.

Section 87103(c) provides that an official has a
financial interest in a governmental decision if the decision
may foreseeably and materially affect a source of income to
the official. In the instant case, Assemblyman Brown has
three possible sources of income - the law firm and the two
physicians in question who are clients of the law firm. The
law firm is a source of income if he receives $250 or more
from the firm in a year. If Assemblyman Brown owns 10 percent
or more of the law firm, a client of the firm also will be a
source of income if Assemblyman Brown's pro rata share of
the client's fees equals $250 or more in a year.

Guidelines for determining when a financial interest
is materially affected are set forth in 2 Cal. Adm. Code
Section 18702. In the instant case, it appears certain that
the board's decision will have a material effect on Assemblyman
Brown's clients since their ability to practice medicine in
the future may be affected. Thus, if a client is a source
of income, it is foreseeable that the board's decision will
affect Assemblyman Brown's financial interests. Even if a
client is not a source of income, the firm will constitute a
financial interest if it is foreseeable that the firm will
be materially affected by the decision. For example, the
board's decision might have a material financial effect on
the firm if it were foreseeable that a client would no longer
retain the firm if the board's decision in this case were
negative.

Floyd Shimomura
January 7, 1977
Page Three

Having concluded that Assemblyman Brown has a
financial interest which could be affected by the board's
decision, we turn now to a discussion of whether his actions
constitute an attempt to use his official position to influence
that decision. Clearly, if he were to appeal before the
board or to communicate with any of its members, either
orally or in writing, for the purpose of assisting his clients,
he would be attempting to influence the board's decision.
Moreover, we think that Assemblyman Brown would be using his
official position to influence a decision if another member
of his firm appeared before the board on behalf of
his agent or as an intermediary. In addition, Assemblyman
Brown would be using his official position to influence the
board's decision if he were aware of and acquiesced in an
attempt by another member of the firm to exploit the rela-
tionship between the Assemblyman and the board.

On the other hand, actions which are undertaken by
another member of the firm which are unknown to Assemblyman
Brown may not be attributed to him. Thus, the actions of
another member of Assemblyman Brown's law firm will not be
attributed to the Assemblyman without some action or acqui-
escence on his part.

Due to the foregoing, Assemblyman Brown's question
concerning whether he may share in compensation paid to his
law firm is academic. As stated above, Assemblyman Brown
may not directly or indirectly use his official position to
influence the board's decision. However, so long as he is
not using his official position to influence, there is no
reason for him not to share in compensation paid to his law
firm. Should you nevertheless decide to discuss the separa-
tion of income in your opinion, our views were previously
submitted to your office in connection with the opinion
requested by Mr. Little of the Coastal Commission (CV 76/203).

If you wish to discuss those matters further,
please contact either Ted Prim or me.

Sincerely,

Michael Bennett
Executive Director

MB:TP:plh

sections.question: Assemblyman Brown wishes to know whether he may represent two physicians before a state board which will consider revocation of the physician's licenses and cancellation of their Medi-Cal providers certificates. If Assemblyman Brown is prohibited from representing the physicians, may another member of the Assemblyman's firm represent them? If so, must Assemblyman Brown amend the law firm's partnership agreement in order to guarantee that he will receive no portion of the physician's payment to the firm.
sections.conclusion: Assemblyman Brown may not directly or indirectly use his official position to influence the board's decision. He may not appear before the board, communicate with members, or have another firm member act on his behalf or as his agent. However, actions by another firm member unknown to Assemblyman Brown and which he does not acquiesce in will not be attributed to him. The question concerning income sharing is academic; if Assemblyman Brown is not using his official position to influence the decision, there is no reason he cannot share in compensation paid to the law firm.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether a state legislator (Assemblyman Brown) may represent physicians before a state board that will decide on license revocation and Medi-Cal provider status, whether a firm member can represent them instead, and whether the legislator must be excluded from sharing compensation if another firm member represents them.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Assemblyman Brown has a financial interest in the board's decision and cannot use his official position to influence it, either directly or indirectly through firm members acting with his knowledge or acquiescence. However, actions by firm members unknown to and not acquiesced in by the Assemblyman will not be attributed to him, and he may share in firm compensation if he is not influencing the decision.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is letter format response from FPPC to Deputy Attorney General; no formal QUESTION/CONCLUSION section headers, but clear question and conclusion present in body text. Reference document CV 76/236 IL; contains extensive citation to Government Code sections and regulations.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Assemblyman Brown wishes to know whether he may represent two physicians before a state board which will consider revocation of the physician's licenses and cancellation of their Medi-Cal providers certificates. If Assemblyman Brown is prohibited from representing the physicians, may another member of the Assemblyman's firm represent them? If so, must Assemblyman Brown amend the law firm's partnership agreement in order to guarantee that he will receive no portion of the physician's payment to the firm.

CONCLUSION: Assemblyman Brown may not directly or indirectly use his official position to influence the board's decision. He may not appear before the board, communicate with members, or have another firm member act on his behalf or as his agent. However, actions by another firm member unknown to Assemblyman Brown and which he does not acquiesce in will not be attributed to him. The question concerning income sharing is academic; if Assemblyman Brown is not using his official position to influence the decision, there is no reason he cannot share in compensation paid to the law firm.

embedding.summary: FPPC advises on whether Assemblyman Brown may represent physicians before a state board considering license revocation when he has a financial interest. The FPPC concludes he cannot directly or indirectly use his official position to influence the decision, but other firm members' actions that he does not know about or acquiesce in may not be attributed to him.
